revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Calendriers Jingū]][[ja:神宮暦]]



 



Jingu Calendars (神宮暦) were historical Japanese calendars people ordered from [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. They showed the days of the year and various astrological things. 

These calendars and their predecessors Ise Calendars 伊勢暦 were the first calendars to be mass produced and distributed in Japan

They had prominent {{ill|Rekichū|ja|暦注|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendrical Notes|qq=}}

==Overview==

'''Jingū Calendar''' (神宮暦) refers to the {{ill|Calendar|en|Calendar|qid=Q12132|lt=calendar|1=Calendar}} produced and distributed by the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}'s {{ill|Jingū Administration Office|ja|神宮司庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Administration Office|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮|1=Ise Grand Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q687168}}.

== Overview ==
Although there is a calendar known as the {{ill|Takaoka Calendar by Jingūkan|ja|神宮館高島暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaoka Calendar by Jingūkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it is unrelated to the Jingū Calendar.

From the Meiji era to the end of World War II, the only booklet-form calendar distributed in Japan was compiled by the government of the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}} and published by the Jingū Administration Office. Formally referred to as the "Honreki" (本暦), it was the official calendar. For information about earlier periods, see the article on {{ill|Japanese calendar|en|Japanese calendar|qid=Q13788|lt=Japanese calendar|1=Japanese calendar}}.

Under the national policy of "Datsu-A Nyū-Ō" (脱亜入欧, "Leave Asia, Enter Europe"), the Meiji government emphasized eliminating superstitions. As a result, all references to traditional calendar notations related to fortune-telling, including {{ill|rokuyō|ja|六曜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rokuyō|qq=}}, were excluded from the Honreki. Instead, the calendar focused on detailed astronomical and meteorological data and listed major shrine festivals. It was a traditional Japanese binding in what would now be considered B4 size. Despite this, {{ill|lunar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lunar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} traditions and {{ill|rokuyō|ja|六曜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rokuyō|qq=}} remained firmly rooted, and these notations continued to appear in the more compact "Ryakuhonreki" (略本暦), a smaller version for everyday use.

The precursor to the Jingū Calendar was the {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}}, published by {{ill|Rekidō|lt=Rekidō|lt=Calendar-makers|ja|暦師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/calendar-makers|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} near the Ise Shrine from the early Edo period. These were distributed nationwide, along with the {{ill|Jingū taima|en|Jingū taima|qid=Q11589469|lt=Jingū taima|1=Jingū taima}}, by the shrine's priests (御師, Onshi).

In 1871 (Meiji 4), the Onshi system was abolished, and distribution of the Ise Calendar ceased. The following year, under the guidance of the Ministry of Education, the {{ill|Calendar Distribution Company|ja|頒暦商社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Calendar Distribution Company|1=Calendar Distribution Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11664874}} was established, bringing together calendar-makers from across Japan. From 1874, the Japanese government exclusively issued and distributed {{ill|official calendars|ja|官暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/official calendars|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, including the Honreki and Ryakuhonreki. In April 1882, an imperial decree mandated that the official calendar be published by the Jingū Administration Office starting in 1883, thus reviving calendar production in Ise.

After 1946 (Shōwa 21), booklet calendars could be freely distributed. Official calendars were subsequently produced by the Tokyo Astronomical Observatory (now the {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|en|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|qid=Q1327713|lt=National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|1=National Astronomical Observatory of Japan}}) and announced in the {{ill|Kanpō (Japanese government gazette)|en|Kanpō (Japanese government gazette)|qid=Q11452096|lt=official gazette|1=Kanpō (Japanese government gazette)}} on February 1 each year.

Today, Ise Shrine continues to issue calendars in the same format as the Honreki and Ryakuhonreki, now called the "Daireki" (大暦) and "Shōreki" (小暦). Collectively, these are referred to as the Jingū Calendar.

== Further reading ==
* {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi |year=1981 |title=日本陰陽道史総説 |trans-title=A Review of the History of Onmyōdō in Japan |url= |language=ja |location= |publisher=Hanawa Shobō |isbn=4827310572|qq=}}
* {{cite book |last=Endō |first=Katsumi |year=1994 |title=近世陰陽道史の研究 |trans-title=Studies in the History of Early Modern Onmyōdō |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher={{ill|Shin Jinbutsu Orai Sha|lt=Shin Jinbutsu Ōrai Sha|ja|新人物往来社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin Jinbutsu Orai Sha|1=Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha. "Rekishi Dokuhon" Henshūbu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11501312}} |isbn=4404021569|qq=}}
* {{cite book |last=Kosaka |first=Shinji |year=2004 |title=安倍晴明撰『占事略決』と陰陽道  |trans-title=Abe no Seimei's "Senji Ryakketsu" and Onmyōdō |url=http://www.kyuko.asia/book/b10889.html |language=Japanese |location= |publisher={{ill|Kyuko Shoin|lt=Kyūko Shoin|ja|汲古書院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyuko Shoin|1=Kyuko Shoin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11552315}} |isbn=9784762941672|qq=}}
* {{cite book |last=Saitō |first=Rei |year=2007 |title=王朝時代の陰陽道 |trans-title=Onmyōdō in the Dynastic Period |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Meicho Kankō Kai |isbn=978-4839003302|qq=}}
* {{cite book |last=Yamashita |first=Katsuaki |year=1996 |title=平安時代の宗教文化と陰陽道 |trans-title=Religious Culture in the Heian Period and Onmyōdō |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Iwata Shoin |isbn=4900697656|qq=}}
* {{cite book |last=Takahashi |first=Keiya |year=2000 |title=現代・陰陽師入門 |trans-title=Introduction to Modern Onmyōji |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=[[Asahi Sonorama]] |isbn=4257035846|qq=}}
* {{cite book |last=Nakamura |first=Shōhachi |year=2000 |title=日本陰陽道書の研究 増補版 |trans-title=A Study of the Onmyō Books in Japan; Expanded Edition |url=http://www.kyuko.asia/book/b10888.html |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Kyūko Shoin |isbn=4257035846|qq=}}
* {{cite book |last=Suzuki |first=Ikkei |year=2002 |title=陰陽道 呪術と鬼神の世界 |trans-title=Onmyōdō: The World of Spells and Demons |url=https://bookclub.kodansha.co.jp/product?item=0000195204 |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=[[Kodansha|Kōdansha]] |isbn=9784062582445|qq=}}
* {{cite book |editor-last=[[Seimei Shrine]] |first= |year=2002 |title=安倍晴明公 |trans-title=The Right Honorable Abe no Seimei |url=https://bookclub.kodansha.co.jp/product?item=0000180822 |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Kōdansha |isbn=9784062109833|qq=}}
* {{cite book |last1=Hayashi |first1=Jun |last2=Koike |first2=Jun'ichi |year=2002 |title=陰陽道の講義 |trans-title=Lecture on Onmyōdō |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Sagano Shoin |isbn=4782303610|qq=}}
* {{cite book |last=Shigeta |first=Shin'ichi |year=2005 |title=平安貴族と陰陽師 |trans-title=Heian Nobles and Onmyōji |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/book/b34635.html |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbun Kan |isbn=4642079424|qq=}}
* {{cite book |last=Hayashi |first=Jun |year=2005 |title=近世陰陽道の研究 |trans-title=A Study of Early Modern Onmyōdō |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/book/b32982.html |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbun Kan |isbn=4642034072|qq=}}
* {{cite book |last=Shigeta |first=Shin'ichi |year=2006 |title=陰陽師 |trans-title=Onmyōji |url=http://www.chuko.co.jp/ebook/2013/07/514036.html |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=[[Chuokoron-Shinsha|Chūōkōron-Shinsha]] |isbn=4121018443|qq=}}
* {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi |year=2017 |title=陰陽道叢書 1 古代 |trans-title=Onmyōdō Series 1: The Ancient Times |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Hanawa Shobō |isbn=978-4626017970|qq=}}
* {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi |year=2017 |title=陰陽道叢書 2 中世 |trans-title=Onmyōdō Series 2: The Middle Ages |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Hanawa Shobō |isbn=978-4626017987|qq=}}
* {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi |year=2017 |title=陰陽道叢書 3 近世 |trans-title=Onmyōdō Series 3: The Early Modern Period |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Hanawa Shobō |isbn=978-4626017994|qq=}}
* {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi |year=2017 |title=陰陽道叢書 4 特論 |trans-title=Onmyōdō Series 4: The Advanced Studies |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Hanawa Shobō |isbn=978-4626018007|qq=}}
